coredata.dc:description: Evaluating human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER2) tumor marker status is important in breast cancer screening, diagnosis, and monitoring. This research uses an electrochemical biosensor, a revolutionary approach in cancer diagnosis, to identify circulating HER2+ tumor cells. The electrochemical activity, shape, size, and morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials are analyzed. A composite comprising nickel-based metal–organic frameworks (MOF), carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanoparticles, and polyethyleneimine (PEI) is coated on the surface of graphite electrodes. After full characterization, this platform improves the surface morphology and properties, electrochemical activity, and biosensing performance. The graphenized graphite electrode-based biosensor demonstrates excellent performance against HER2-overexpressing SK-BR-3 cancer cells, with a linear dynamic range of 100–500 cells per mL and an analytical detection limit of less than 1 cell/ mL. Accordingly, the benefits of this highly efficient platform include high specificity, wide dynamic range, reproducibility, selectivity, and low cost. These characteristics indicate that the fabricated nanobiosensor has high potential for monitoring and detecting HER2 levels in breast cancer patients and clinical diagnosis.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All values are presented as initially published. Units differ based on the specific analyte targeted (e.g., protein concentration versus cell count). Our approach relies on the direct quantification of SK-BR3 cells (cells/mL), in contrast to others that often identify indicators such as proteins or nucleic acids (pg/mL, ng/mL).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Evaluating human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER2) tumor marker status is important in breast cancer screening, diagnosis, and monitoring. This research uses an electrochemical biosensor, a revolutionary approach in cancer diagnosis, to identify circulating HER2+ tumor cells. The electrochemical activity, shape, size, and morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials are analyzed. A composite comprising nickel-based metal–organic frameworks (MOF), carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanoparticles, and polyethyleneimine (PEI) is coated on the surface of graphite electrodes. After full characterization, this platform improves the surface morphology and properties, electrochemical activity, and biosensing performance. The graphenized graphite electrode-based biosensor demonstrates excellent performance against HER2-overexpressing SK-BR-3 cancer cells, with a linear dynamic range of 100–500 cells per mL and an analytical detection limit of less than 1 cell/ mL. Accordingly, the benefits of this highly efficient platform include high specificity, wide dynamic range, reproducibility, selectivity, and low cost. These characteristics indicate that the fabricated nanobiosensor has high potential for monitoring and detecting HER2 levels in breast cancer patients and clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In cancer diseases cells divide uncontrollably, causing the tumors formation. Additionally, tumor cells can metastasize to further organs through blood vessels, extending the disease to other body parts[1]. Globally, breast cancer (BC) is the most prevalent kind of cancer in women, standing as the fourth leading cause of cancer deaths in the world [2]. Breast cancer treatment relies on early detection and monitoring, with imaging methods being a powerful tool for early diagnosis and treatment response, preventing tumor progression and preventing progression in healthy individuals[3]. The use of different imaging techniques is associated with several challenges, such as high cost, insufficiency of sensitivity, and specificity. Hence, it seems necessary to introduce new biosensors that can overcome the limitations associated with imaging techniques. Sensors have been developed using analytical technologies to quickly monitor and analyze human health. Optical sensors use light interaction to generate measurable signals, such as absorption, fluorescence scattering, surface plasmon resonance spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. Electrochemical sensors detect electrical properties associated with redox reactions, using electrodes in electrolyte solutions. Bioimpedance sensors measure tissue resistance to electrical current flow, using single-frequency bioimpedance and multi-frequency bioimpedance. These sensors are versatile and powerful tools for tissue analysis and pharmaceutical quality control[4]. The use of different biomarkers is one of the vital parts of the monitoring and diagnosis of breast cancer patients. Suitable biomarkers can help to understand better the cell and molecular pathways that affect the pathogenesis of breast cancer, design an effective treatment approach, and monitor the response to therapy in patients with this cancer[5]. Other biological fluids, such as sputum and urine, also contain breast cancer biomarkers in addition to blood[6].The use of different biomarkers is distinguished as one of thesignificantelements of monitoring and diagnosis of breast cancer patients. Many studies have shown that different biochemical markers, including mRNAs, proteins, microRNAs, and enzymes, canbe biomarkers for breast cancer diagnosis and treatment[7]. Non-invasive approaches are necessary to enhance early detection in the analysis of biological fluids[8,9]. An uncomplicated non-invasive blood screening assay could identify and track cancer advancement, thereby alleviating patient suffering. Nonetheless, there are limited blood-based biomarkers with clinical use in breast cancer screening[10].

A variety of biochemical markers, such as proteins like HER2, ER, and Ki67, mRNAs like ER, ER, and ERR, enzymes like TSGF and CEA, and microRNAs like miR-145, miR-10b, miR-21, and miR-155, can serve as diagnostic biomarkers for the diagnosis and treatment of breast cancer patients, according to several studies. Biomarkers aid in managing breast cancer by assessing the biological status of a disorder, identifying the type of tumor, tracking its progression, and monitoring its response to treatment[11]. Various tissues express human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), which is responsible for normal cell growth in non-cancerous cells. Excessive HER2 levels in cancer cells lead to rapid tumor growth. About 15–20 % of breast cancers are HER2 positive, with one in five women having HER2-positive breast cancer, which can be more aggressive than some HER2-negative breast cancers[3,12,13].

Monitoring HER2 cells is very necessary because it is one of the significant biomarkers for breast cancer diagnosis [14]. To improve the sensing performance (including sensitivity, response speed, and selectivity) and the ease of biosensor fabrication, many researchers have used carbon-based nanomaterials, conducting metal–organic frameworks, quantum dots, polymers, noble metals, and metal oxides[15,16 17].

Biomolecules, attached to signal transducers, aid in the determination and diagnosis of target analytes in bioassays. The immobilization of biomolecules is crucial for biosensing with a high implementation lifetime. Substrate materials like nanoparticles, electrodes, nanotubes, and mesoporous materials should be modified to provide functional groups that bind to biomolecules with long-term stability, bond strength, activity, and biocompatibility. Sensitive cancer identification and diagnosis are important for early detection[18,19].

Sensitive identification and diagnosis of cancer is of particular value because of the prompt need for fast and efficient methods of early diagnosis in cancer diseases. But, since there are very lowconcentrations of circulating cancer cells in the peripheral blood,the tumor cells detection with high sensitivity and specificity remains challenging [20,21].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have exhibited effective potential in different fields due to their extensive specific surface areas and high porosity. In addition, MOFs can interact with biomarkers by different mechanisms that provide high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting cancer biomarkers. For example, Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), oxidation–reduction, electron, energy, and proton transfer are common mechanisms describing signal changes. Nevertheless, the instability and poor electrical conductivity of MOFs in aqueous solution restrict their additional application in electrochemical sensors [9,22,23].The amalgamation of MOFs with sophisticated functional materials has attracted significant interest due to enhanced electrical conductivity and chemical stability[24]. Incorporating guest species in MOFs enhances stability and longevity. Biomolecules that introduce new functional groups can further enhance hydrogen bonding interactions with guest biomarkers. Host-guest strategies for synthesizing nucleic acid@MOFs, antibody@MOFs, enzymes@MOFs, and biomacromolecules@MOFs have improved biosensing applications in cancer biomarker detection, reducing the cost of rapid target cancer biomarker detection.

MOFs doped with quantum dots can optimize their performance for sensor and biosensor applications. These strategies can improve stability, catalytic activity, and selectivity in biosensing areas. The confinement of metal nanoparticles within the MOF framework prevents aggregation and controls the size and shape of immobilized nanoparticles, allowing for precise sensing properties[25]. Nickel-based metal–organic frameworks (Ni-MOFs) are promising porous materials for electrochemical sensing due to their high surface area, structural tunability, and catalytic properties. To overcome these limitations: (1) structural optimization and ligand engineering, (2) forming composites with conductive materials (e.g., metal oxides, carbon, or polymers), and (3) converting them into derivatives through calcination or solution-based processes. These approaches enhance conductivity, dispersion, and stability[26,27].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could be used for fast electrochemical reactions because they are biocompatible, chemically stable, easy to make, conduct electricity well, and have surface activities that can be change [28,29]. CQDs greatly enhance the electron transfer output and electrocatalytic activity of the materials they are attached to because they accept electrons. Consequently, the synergistic interaction between the abundant active metal sites of porous MOFs and the elevated electrical conductivity of CQDs can stimulate this electrocatalytic activity[30,31]. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), an ionized amino group polymer, has high conductivity, excellent water solubility, and significant biological affinity. PEI has been employed to alter MOFs and enhance their electrochemical characteristics[32]. In recent studies, PEI significantly reduced the ion–electron transfer distance and increased the conductivity of the material. Accordingly, PEI modification of MOFs can increase their conductivity[33]. Wireless biosensors enable real-time, continuous monitoring of cancer biomarkers through flexible, minimally invasive platforms, integrating advanced materials with wireless transmission technologies for enhanced diagnostic accuracy[4].

In this study, we investigated an electrochemical biosensing platform and developed a novel, effective method for modifying the graphite electrode surface using GO, rGO, and polyethylenimine@carbon quantum dots@Ni-metal–organic frameworks functionalized with HER2 + to identify and quantify SK-BR-3 cells. To the best of our knowledge, PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF has not yet been used in biosensor development through a novel Ni-based metal–organic framework synthesis method, particularly for breast cancer detection.

4-amino-5-phthalic acid, diazabicyclo, Nickel nitrate hexahydrate, diammonium Hydrogen Citrate, Potassium Ferricyanide, Potassium Ferrocyanide, Potassium Chloride, Dimethylformamide (All from Merck, Germane), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide, Glutaraldehyde, Bovine serum albumin, Phosphate-buffered saline, PEI (All from Sigma Aldrich, United States America), Ethanol (Kimia alcohol, Iran), DMEM/F12 medium (Inoclon, Iran), Trypsin EDTA (Gibco Invitrogen, Germane), Herceptin(AryoTrust (Trastuzumab), Iran).
All of the cell lines (The SKBR3, MCF-7, k562, L929, and MDA-MB231 cell lines) and plasmas were provided by Motamed Cancer Institute, Tehran, Iran.

X-ray diffractometer (bourevestnik DRON-8), electrochemical sensor (Palm sense 3), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (MIRA III-TESCAN), Raman (N1-541 T), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (PerkinElmer), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BELSORP MINI II), Transmission Imaging data were processed using ImageJ 1.52v (NIH, USA) for TEM analysis.
https://imagej.net/ij/index.htmL.
The Origin Pro 2024 v10.1.0.178 (SR1) was used for the analysis and plotting of XRD, Raman, and FTIR data.(https://www.originlab.com/index.aspx?go=Products/Origin).

XRD patterns were analyzed using Xpert High score Plus, v3.0e (3.0.5) (Malvern Panalytical, Netherlands) to determine the crystallographic properties and phase composition.(https://www.malvernpanalytical.com/en/products/category/software/x-ray-diffraction-software/highscore-with-plus-option).
Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 10.4.0.621 (GraphPad Software, USA).(https://www.graphpad.com/updates/prism-10–4-0-release-notes).

Electrochemical measurements were recorded and analyzed using PSTace 4.8 software (PalmSens BV, Netherlands) to evaluate specific analysis, current density, etc. https://pstrace.software.informer.com/4.8/.

First, 2 g of di-ammonium hydrogen citrate powder was added to 75 mL of pure water and stirred for 15 min at room temperature (RT). After dissolving this powder in pure water, the solution was poured into a Teflon autoclave container (hydrothermal reactor) and was heated at 130° Celsius for 18 h. In this synthesis, carbon quantum dots with a concentration of 26.6 g/L were obtained[34,35].

66 Mg of 1,4 diazabicyclo[2.2.2]-octane, with 220 mg of 5-amino isophthalic acid were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF and stirred for 15 min at RT. Then Nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate was dissolved in pure water. This solution and carbon quantum dots solution in another balloon were dispersed by ultrasonic homogenizer in the presence of water and ice bath for 10 min to prevent accumulation and sedimentation of particles. Finally, the second solution was combined with the first one dropwise and stirred for 30 min. Then it was poured into a Teflon autoclave container and heated in the oven for 24 h at a temperature of 130° Celsius. The obtained dark green solution was washed with DMF and ethyl alcohol twice for each by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min and then dried at 60° Celsius under vacuum

15 mL of Ni-MOF@CQD was dissolved in 3 mL of PEI (1 % W/V) and stirred for 12 h. Subsequently, it was placed inside a dialysis bag for 3 days. After three days, the solution was sonicated using an ultrasonic probe to ensure complete dispersion

The enhanced performance of the developed immunosensor is due to a synergy of higher electron transfer, elevated surface conductivity, and efficient biorecognition interactions. The alteration of the graphite electrode with PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF and reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles (rGONPs) markedly improved the electrochemical surface area, resulting in elevated peak current densities in CV and DPV, attributable to accelerated redox probe diffusion and charge transfer. The PEI component, possessing ionizable amino groups, enhanced electrochemical conductivity and offered several anchoring sites for antibody immobilization. The covalent attachment of anti-HER2 antibodies by EDC/NHS chemistry facilitated the selective capture of SKBR3 cells. Following antigen–antibody interaction, the negatively charged SKBR3 cells obstructed the redox probe's access to the electrode surface, leading to a reduction in peak currents, which serve as a metric for quantifying cell concentration. The mechanistic basis is thus founded on a synthesis of signal attenuation resulting from steric and electrostatic influences during cell attachment, alongside effective electron transfer facilitated by the nanocomposite-modified surface.

All electrochemical methods of analysis, including CV and DPV were accomplished via an adaptation voltage of 30 in. Palmsens BV PGSTAT potentiostat–galvanostat, via a standard three-cell electrode. Additionally, graphite was used as a working electrode. A suitable potential range was measured against Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl solution as a reference electrode. A platinum wire electrode was utilized as a counter electrode. CV curves were reported at 50 mV/s scan rate and in −0.2 V to 0.6 V versus Ag/AgCl. Besides, DPV was incorporated in the range of −0.2 to 0.6, with a pulse width of 0.2 in. and an amplitude of 50 millivolts. SWV was also measured in the range of −0.2 to 0.8 V with an amplitude of 50 millivolts and a frequency of 25 Hz[36].

At first, 372.5 mg of potassium chloride was dissolved in 50 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4). After that, 82.5 mg of Potassium Ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6) and 92 mg of Potassium Ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6) were introduced to the solution and stirred for 20 min.

Before analysis, the graphite electrodes were divided into three cm pieces. The tips of the graphite electrodes were polished by a 3000-grit sandpaper to remove a considerable amount of graphite before polishing. It was stretched and cleaned on a microfiber textile moistened with absolute ethyl alcohol followed by pure water prior to the electrochemical treatment, and finally, the polished head was covered with Teflon tape to create an insulating area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Converting graphite electrode surface to graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite oxidation to form nanosheets of graphene oxide (GO) and reducing these sheets to reduced graphene oxide (rGO) is an efficient method to increase electrolyte diffusion, surface charge transfer, and reactivity on the electrode surface. We utilized a straightforward, efficient, and innovative in situ redox electrochemical method to synthesize rGONPs-modified graphite electrode (GE) directly.CV with a scanning speed of 50 mV/s and in 0 to 3 V with four sequential movements in PBS (pH = 6.6) was used to fabricate multilayered GO nanosheets on the electrode surface. After that, CV was done with a 50 mV/s scan rate and potential of 0 to −1.6 V in the same PBS for the electrochemical reduction of GO nanosheets in four consecutive moves to obtain a graphite electrode modified with GO nanosheets[37–39].

After preparing the graphite electrode surface, the surface was incubated with 20 Âµl of a solution having 7 mg of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), 12 mg of NHS, and 0.5 mL of pure water for one hour. It was incubated on the surface at a temperature of 4° Celsius. EDC is a water-soluble and cross-linking agent that creates amide bonds between amine and carboxyl groups. However, EDC is very unstable in water. The use of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) is preferred to boost the EDC's stability. This solution has been used to cross-link antibodies to the functionalized solid support surface. After one hour, the electrode head was washed with half a milliliter of pure water, and then 20 Âµg of the sonicated CQD@Ni-MOF solution was applied on the application surface and then was incubated for 12 h at 4° Celsius. After 12 h, it was washed with PBS (PH = 7.4), and then 12 Âµl of glutaraldehyde solution were applied on the surface for one hour. After one hour, the electrodes were washed with 0.5 ml of PBS (PH = 7.4), and 20 Âµl of HER2 + antibody (Ab) were placed on the surface of the electrode for 6 h and kept at 4° Celsius. After washing with PBS (PH = 7.4), 20 Âµl of bovine albumin (BSA) with a concentration of 5 % was applied on the surface of Ab@CQD@Ni-MOF@rGONPs@graphite electrode and incubated for 5 min at 4° Celsius to block those activated functional groups of carboxyl on the modified electrode surface that does not bond with the HER2 antibody amino-functional groups. Finally, the functionalized electrode was washed several times using PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performing tests related to determining the concentration and incubation time of antibodies
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.5 ml of 0.022 mg/ mL antibody was used. It was diluted with PBS (pH = 7.4) to obtain 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 62 μg/ mL antibody concentrations and they were stored at 4 °C. After polishing the electrodes, applying the sample, and incubating for the desired time, the electrode head was washed with PBS followed by applying 20 Âµl of antibody on the electrode surface. After the desired time, it was washed with PBS and finally, DPV and CV tests were performed in ferroferric cyanide solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conducting experiments related to different concentrations of SKBR3 cell line
ce:section.ce:para: First, the graphite electrode preparation, including polishing, sample application, and antibody application, was performed. After washing the electrode containing bovine albumin with phosphate buffer saline (pH = 7.4), 20 Âµl of different concentrations of cell lines (50,100, 300, 500, 1000 cells/mL) were applied on the surface of the electrode and incubated for 20 min. It was incubated at RT with the cover to prevent drying. After 20 min, it was washed with phosphate buffer and placed in a ferroferric cyanide solution. Then CV and DPV tests were taken.

At this stage, the selectivity of the antibody used for SKBR3 and not for other analytes in the body was tested using several types of analytes. For this purpose, different cell lines such as myelogenous leukemia cell line (k562), breast cancer cell line (MCF-7), breast cancer cell line (MDA), breast cancer cell line (SKBR3), and fibroblast cell line (L929) with a specific concentration of 100 cells/mL were placed on the surface of the prepared electrodes. After 20 min, the electrode surface was washed using phosphate buffer saline and placed in a ferroferric cyanide solution. Next, relevant tests were performed. The SKBR3, MCF-7, k562, L929, and MDA-MB231 cell lines were acquired from the Motamed Cancer Institute in Iran.

The cell line of SK-BR3 and its products are used as a positive control in the detection of HER2 + tumor markers. SK-BR3 in DMEM, 1 % penicillin–streptomycin (antibiotic), and 10 % FBS at 37° Celsius in an incubator with 95 % humidity and 5 % CO2 were cultured. After three days, the cells were harvested by trypsin/ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA). Finally, about 20 Âµg of the pipetted cells were applied on the modified electrode surface and incubated for 20 min at 37° Celsius. To electrochemically determine the cancer cells concentration on the electrode, the nano biosensor electric current signal in Ferroferrycyanide buffer as a redox probe was measured by DPV and CV methods. The changes in current density (Δj) versus different cancer cell concentrations were considered analytical signals.

FTIR spectroscopy investigations led to the identification of functional groups. Fig. 1 B(a) shows the normal O-H stretching vibration of water molecules, which occurs in the broad absorption band between 3200 and 3500 , suggesting the presence of adsorbed or coordinated water molecules in the Ni-MOF structure. The peaks at 1673–1534 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C=C. Additionally, we perceive the peaks at 720 to 781 as a vibration of C-H[40]. The symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of the coordinated –COO– groups, are responsible for the strong bands at 1316 and 1645 in turn, suggesting that the –COO– of H2BDC coordinates with through a double ligand mode[41]. Finally, the CQD spectrum attributed the absorption peak at 1395, 1458, 1513, and 1645 to C=C and C=O stretching vibrations, COO-symmetric stretching vibrations, and C-O stretching vibrations, in turn (Fig. 1B(b))[31,42]. The bands at 1673 and 1380 related to the N–H bend vibration and groups originating from the PEI compound[43,44]. Furthermore, the spectral range from around 2930 exhibits the presence of C–H stretching and bending vibration peaks associated with groups in PEI. The peaks in this area, including a peak at around 1534 , were clearly and distinctly visible for the PEI because of the high concentration of the group is shown in Fig. 1B(c)[45].

Fig. 1(A) displays the XRD pattern. The XRD pattern of the Ni-MOF and CQD@Ni-MOF, closely resembles the main diffraction peaks and it can be seen that adding CQD did not affect the structure of the Ni-MOF. However, the solvent affects the size and stability of the crystals, resulting in a slight change in the position, width, and intensity of several peaks. Furthermore, polyethyleneimine (PEI), being a polymeric substance, is intrinsically amorphous and lacks identifiable or sharp diffraction peaks in XRD examination. This is a recognized trait of polymers, which typically exhibit broad, diffuse halos rather than distinct crystalline peaks[46,47]. Diffraction peaks (CCDC no. 964304872) at 7.8°, 9.5°, 14.5°, 17.93°, and 23.9° correspond to planes (011), (100), (002), (211), and (21–1). The diffraction peaks were indexed to the typical crystal structure of CQD@Ni-MOF similar to the characteristic peaks of crystalline MOF. The 7.8° peak, which corresponds to the 011 plane, increased, indicating an improvement in crystallinity[34,35,48].

FESEM characterized the particle size distribution, morphology, and microstructure of the CQD@Ni-MOF sample. The CQD@Ni-MOF sample has an average particle size of 126 nm having a shell structure having a crystalline structure, which can improve its electrochemical properties (Fig. 2 A). Homogeneous and narrow particle size distributions can positively affect the application of MOF nanostructures. After the characterization, a cubic (two-dimensional) layered structure with many pores was also shown, which increases charge and ion transfer The field emission scanning electron microscopy picture of the PEI@CQD@MOF nanomaterials (Fig. 2B) also showed a structure that was similar to the bare nickel-based metal–organic framework. The average diameter of the framework was 155 nm, which was a bit bigger than plain MOF. These results demonstrate that the incorporation of CQDs and PEI does not destroy the nickel-based framework's crystal structure but rather strengthens it[49,50].

Raman spectroscopy was used to examine the characterization of PEI@CQD@MOF, and CQD@Ni-MOF. The peak locations were seen at wave numbers 802, 999, 1276, 1371, 1418, 1449, 1522, 1614, and 2955 . The perspectives were derived from the information cited in the sources. Peaks at 802, 999, 1276, 1418, 1449, and 1614 are associated with Ni-MOF. According to Fig. 3 A, the peak at 802 corresponds to the out-of-plane bending of C-H in the benzene ring. The carboxylate stretching is associated with the peaks at 999 and 1449 , which are characteristic peaks of Ni-MOF. The peak at 1276 corresponds to C-N stretching. The peak at the 1418 position is related to the symmetric stretching of carboxylate, and the peak at the 1614 position indicates the vibration of the benzene ring. Carbon quantum dots relate to the peaks at positions 1522 , and 2955 . N-H bending modes are observed in 1371 , which indicates the PEI presence. The peak at position 1522 signifies the quantum dot's degree of graphiticity, while the peak at position 2955 signifies the presence of order in a broad spectrum of matter, stemming from a two-technique interaction process[45,51–53].

BET analysis investigated changes in pore size, pore diameter, and specific surface area after CQD and PEI incorporation. Fig. 3B shows the pore size distribution of plain MOF and PEI@CQD@MOF. The average size of the pores in the Ni-MOF sample and PEI@CQD@MOF shrunk from 7.99 nm to 1.12 nm, and the Ni-MOF surface area and PEI@CQD@MOF shrunk from 4.7925 to 2.0452 . In general, when CQD and PEI were added to the metal–organic framework, the specific surface area and pore diameter went down. This is because the CQD and PEI filled up the metal–organic framework's pores. Metal-organic frameworks have porous structures with well-defined pores and channels. These pores can encapsulate CQD and PEI, enhancing electron transport, ion diffusion, and electrochemical performance[31,41,54].

The illustration shows that the Ni-MOF particles form stacked, nearly planar sheets (Fig. 4 A and 4B). This characteristic indicates metal–organic frameworks and signifies a layered architecture including discrete sheets. The enlarged TEM pictures show the loose yet systematic arrangement of multiple thin sheets to form a nearly regular rectangular cubic structure[54,55].The presence of CQDs in the CQD@MOF nanohybrid was verified through TEM, as shown in Fig. 4C. These TEM images further confirm the nanosheet microstructures of CQD@MOF with few layers[56].

TEM was used to analyze the morphology, dimensions, and microstructure of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 4D,4E). The TEM image in Fig. 4F reveals that the CQDs exhibit a consistent almost spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution[34,57]. Statistical analysis of particles reveals that the average size of synthesized CQDs is about 28.013 nm. Also The size distributions of CQDs are in the range of 16.313–44.041 nm.

DPV, SWV, and CV are important techniques utilized to improve immunosensors and test how well they work. Fig. 4B displays the CVs of several modified Graphite electrodes (GE). The electrochemical achievement of the modified GE is compared to that of the unmodified GE. Biosensor development frequently uses the CV approach because it can provide crucial insights into redox processes and their reversibility in reactions. The peak current densities of the PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF/ rGONPs/ GE are comparatively greater than those of rGONPs/graphite and bare GE. This increased conductivity in the presence of PEI-NPs, CQD-NPs, and Ni-MOF and the increased diffusive mass transfer of -anion to the surface of PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF, rGONPs, and GE compared to other unmodified surfaces were responsible for this enhancement in the electrochemical signal. The electrochemical properties of PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF /rGONPs/graphite electrodes were analyzed in a PBS solution having 5.0 mM potassium ferri-/ferro-cyanide and 0.1 M of KCl as a reference electrochemical probe solution. Fig. 5 A and 5B displays the DPV and CV of both modified and unmodified GE. Modifications using rGONPs and PEI@CQD@NiMOF enhance peak current densities. DPV is advantageous for quantitative determination because it has a lower detection limit and a higher signal-to-noise ratio.

Because of its high conductivity, excellent water solubility, and strong biological affinity, polyethyleneimine, a polymer with ionized amino groups, improves the electrochemical characteristics of MOFs[58]. Optimizing the polyethyleneimine concentration on the graphite electrode surface modified with CQD@Ni-MOF using DPV, CV, and SWV in ferrocyanide buffer in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.6 V and using 5 different concentrations (Fig. 6 A-C). According to Fig. 6D, the current intensity increased with the rise of the concentration of polyethyleneimine on the electrode surface reaching the value of 400 nM and then remained constant. This constant current difference is due to the saturation of the surface with polyethyleneimine. To get the best incubation time, the sample was tested on the electrode surface for 3, 6, 9, and 12 h, as shown in Fig. 7 A and 7B. As the electrode surface saturated after 9 h of incubation, the intensity of Δj enhanced with the rise of contact time and remained almost constant. Therefore, the optimal time of incubation was determined to be 9 h (Fig. 7C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization and optimization of HER2 antibody on modified electrode surface
ce:section.ce:para: 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide/N-hydroxysuccinimide (EDC/NHS) reaction was used for covalent crosslinking, immobilization of antibodies on the surface of the modified electrode, and activation of carboxyl groups. The non-electrical nature of the antibody(Ab) biomolecule, along with the presence of phosphate groups in its structure, ionizes it to many negative charges in the aqueous solution. This strong electrostatic repulsion with ferrocyanide ion redox prevents electron transfer on the surface of the electrode. This bio-immune sensor exhibited a significant reduction in peak current measurements of CV and DPV. The variation in electrochemical current density resulting from changes in oncomarker concentration, as contrasted to the stability achieved by BSA, was utilized as the measuring system denoted as Δj. The impact of two crucial experimental parameters of HER2 + antibody concentration and its binding time to the modified electrode surface was investigated. Utilizing GA and supports with main amino groups is a commonly employed technique for immobilizing antibodies30. First, graphite electrodes modified with PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF were activated by glutaraldehyde, and then different concentrations of anti-HER2 antibody (4000, 2000, 1000, 500, 250 μg/mL) with the presence of 100 cells/mL were placed on the electrode surface as shown in Fig. 8 A-CAs shown in Fig. 8D, increasing the concentration of HER2 + antibody up to 2000 μg/mL boosted the current density. Nonetheless, no significant changes were detected for antibody concentrations higher than 2000 μg/mL because the modified electrode surface was completely saturated with the antibodies. Based on this, the concentration of 2000 μg/mL antibody was chosen as the optimal concentration. The antibody incubation on the modified electrode surface was also evaluated at lower antibody concentrations. It was observed that the selected concentration was appropriately chosen. To this end, DPV tests were conducted at antibody concentrations of 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 62 μg/mL (Fig. 9 D).

The strength of the binding between the antibody and the target antigen determines the duration of incubation. The incubation rate of the antigen–antibody complex has an impact on determining the reaction time of the biosensor. This parameter directly affects the performance of the immunosensor[59]. Also, to obtain the most potent binding time of the HER2 + antibody, a test was taken for 3, 6, 9, and 12 h according to Fig. 9B and Fig. 9C. The intensity of the j boosted with a rise in contact time and remained almost constant after 9 h of incubation due to the fact that the electrode surface was saturated. Thus, the optimal incubation time was determined to be 6 h (Fig. 9A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensing performance of immunosensor toward HER2+ oncomarker
ce:section.ce:para: This stage of the test was performed to ensure the binding of the SKBR3 analyte to its specific antibody, and to determine the detection range and the lowest detection limit of this nanobioimmunosensor, using different concentrations of the analyte and electrochemical tests. These tests were performed for concentrations of 1000, 500, 300, 150, 100, and 50 cells/mL of SKBR3 in order from low to high concentration. The findings show that as the concentration of the analyte rises, the electrochemical peak decreases. This is because as the concentration of the analyte rises, the resistance to electron transfer rises and the maximum current falls in each step. Also, calibration curves have been drawn to check the linearity of the bioimmunosensor response and calculate its detection limit. We assessed the constructed safety sensor's efficiency under optimal settings using CV and DPV techniques. In the DPV method, the linear relationship between Δj and Log [C] has been shown in Fig. 10 A and B. The LOD calculated using this equation is 1 cell/mL. Hence, the findings indicate that the immunosensor performs well even when the SKBR3 concentrations are low. Many types of cancer may use this immunosensor to detect minor fluctuations in oncomarker levels at an early stage of the illness. Similarly, CV were employed to assess the LOD of the PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF−based sensor for HER2 + cells and determine its performance. We observe that an increase in SK-BR-3 cells leads to an increase in immunoconjugates on the immunosensor, which gradually obstructs the redox ferrocyanide probe. Inset of Fig. 10C as a result, the peak current of the CV decreased, which led to the occurrence of an off-path signal. Further, the regression equations Δj (µA) and and were described with a limit of LOD of 1 cell/mL.

Selectivity and responsiveness are the two key factors determining a biosensor's ability to detect electroactive substances in real biospecimens[60]. The selectivity of the biosensor was evaluated by testing it against other cancer cells that may be present in the blood. Five cancer cells were used in specific concentrations of 100 cells/mL. To find the parameter ΔJ/J, we divide the difference in intensity between the DPV peaks by the main intensity (before cell implantation). We do this by leaving out the electrode difference parameter from the results of the selectivity analysis. These cells include the myelogenous leukemia cell line (K562), breast cancer (MCF-7), carcinoma (MDA-MB231), breast cancer cell line (SKBR3), and fibroblast cell line (L929). Fig. 11 A and B present a comprehensive comparison of the entire dataset, including the relevant curves. When comparing the outputs of the other cell lines, it is evident that SK-BR-3 cells have a ΔJ/J ratio that is higher than 0.55. The obtained results demonstrate the high selectivity of this bioimmunosensor to the HER2 + breast cancer antigen by comparing its response to K562, MCF-7, MDA, and L929 with its response to the SK-BR-3 analyte. Simply put, SKBR3 cells showed a stronger link with the modified electrode surfaces than with other cells. The modest levels of HER2 expression in MDA-MB231, MCF-7, L929, K562, and cells are the reason for this. Furthermore, the anti-HER2 probe can capture SKBR3 cells due to their high HER2 antigen content. Hence, the engineered nanocomposite demonstrates a robust attraction and specificity towards SKBR3 cells.

The reproducibility or precision of the immunosensor for inter-assay was assessed by measuring the SKBR3 cells (100 cell/mL) using four separate electrodes (Fig. 11C). The peak currents of the four changed electrodes exhibited a high degree of similarity, with an acceptable relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.8. Considering that the RSD is below 1.9 %, which is the recommended reference value by the Association of Analytical Communities (AOAC), it can be concluded that the measurements conducted using the PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF/ rGONPs/ GE biosensor exhibit a high level of reproducibility33. Fig. 11D displays a FESEM image of immobilized SKBR3 cells on the surface of the electrode.

Spherical cells were found of varying sizes, with slender extensions and membrane flaps. In vitro, these cells exhibited a round shape, small diameter, and cluster-like growth pattern. Strong binding and adhesion of cells to the electrode surface is visible[61,62].

As seen in Fig. 12 C, comparing the signal changes after 20 days to the first day with an RSD of 3.01 % shows that the biosensor has kept up its ability to detect and stay stable. As can be seen from Fig. 12A and 12B, the developed biosensor is highly reliable and efficient.

This study did not encompass comprehensive investigations into the impacts of varying pH levels or potential confounding agents; however, the sensor was evaluated in a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) environment at a near-physiological pH of 7.4, reflective of biological fluids. Under these conditions, the immunosensor demonstrated exceptional stability and dependable detection of HER2 + antibodies. The materials utilized in the immunosensor, specifically Ni-MOF, carbon quantum dots, and polyethyleneimine, have been thoroughly documented to exhibit chemical and electrochemical stability under physiological settings[31,63,64]. Furthermore, previous research suggests that similar electrochemical sensing platforms exhibit significant selectivity and negligible interference from biological substances, including glucose, ascorbic acid, and uric acid in buffered solutions. Consequently, the engineered immunosensor is anticipated to preserve its efficacy and stability in biologically pertinent settings [31,65].

In addition, the analytical characteristics of this biosensor was compared with that of the previously reported biosensor, shown in Table 1 . As summarized in Table 1, It is important to note that the analyte used in this study was whole SKBR3 cells, unlike most previous studies that targeted soluble tumor biomarkers. Consequently, the concentration units in this study are denoted in cells/mL, while other studies present detection limits in ng/mL or pg/mL. Despite the inability to conduct a direct comparison owing to the varying analyte types, the suggested sensor attained a detection limit of 1 cell/mL, which is notably competitive within the realm of cell-based biosensing. Furthermore, the sensor presents numerous benefits, such as a label-free design, straightforward preparation, affordability, and elevated sensitivity, rendering it a potential option for the early identification of cancer cells in simulated clinical specimens. Combining Ni-MOFs with CQD and PEI increases the surface area and conductivity, which improves the platform's sensitivity and specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical quantificative determination of SKBR3 cells in the simulated human serum
ce:section.ce:para: 10 % FBS, 100 U /mL penicillin, and 100 mg /mL streptomycin were added to DMEM media to culture the SKBR3 cells. The cells were incubated at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Cancer cells were passaged using trypsin, and 100, 150, 300, and 500 cells were spiked in 20 Âµl of the simulated human serum (synthetic serum), and healthy human blood serum was used as a control and analyzed four times (Fig. 12D).

We evaluated the precision of the outlined sensing technique by spiking blank samples with four distinct doses of the target antigen. The acquired data demonstrate a high accuracy in synthetic serum samples, indicating that this immunosensor can effectively measure the specified tumor markers in synthetic serum samples (Table 2 ).

The development of an inexpensive, uncomplicated, and rapid technique for early cancer detection is an urgent concern in the field of health and medicine. Electrochemical methods are superior to other diagnostic methods in terms of high sensitivity, rapid response, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendliness. The Ni-based metal–organic framework, as previously mentioned, possesses distinct physicochemical properties that enable the fabrication of biosensors. The inclusion of carbon quantum dots and polyethyleneimine in the material increases its conductivity, thereby greatly improving the biosensor's performance and sensitivity. Consequently, methods for early disease detection, including some types of cancer, can be greatly improved by using nickel-based metal–organic frameworks together with carbon quantum dots and polyethyleneimine or antibodies. Breast cancer was diagnosed in this study by measuring the quantity of breast cancer antigen using an immunosensor. Given that one of the key features of sensor selection is the suitable detection limit and its accessibility, an effort was made to boost the productivity of breast cancer immunosensors by choosing relevant parameters. Given that the suitable detection limit and its accessibility are among the key features of sensor selection, an effort was made to boost the productivity of breast cancer immunosensors by choosing relevant parameters. This study presents a special type of electrochemical immunosensor that can detect SKBR3 breast cancer cells without needing labels, using a Ab@PEI@CQD@Ni-MOF nanocomposite. The combination of nickel-based MOFs with conductive carbon quantum dots and polyethyleneimine greatly enhanced the sensor's ability to conduct electricity, its compatibility with biological materials, and its overall performance in analysis. The new sensor could detect as few as 1 cell/mL and showed great consistency in its results (RSD = 1.8 %), with a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.96) over a wide range of concentrations. Our method presents various benefits over previously reported sensors, including lower cost, quicker response, greater sensitivity, and simplicity of fabrication, making it an attractive option for point-of-care cancer diagnosis. These results highlight the potential of modified MOF-based systems to support new biosensors for medical use and early disease detection.
